## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Design-locked from an extended design session; multi-step — decompose into sub-paces at groom. Mechanical once the locked decisions are held. Gated: do not start until ₣BK closes.
  
  ## Goal
  
  Replace the hand-maintained bash↔Rust constant mirrors with build-time generation from the canonical bash source, so cross-language drift becomes structurally impossible for everything projected. (Today the colophons are a hand-copied Rust mirror guarded only by a runtime assertion — `rbtdrm_verify` against the zipper's manifest string — which detects drift but does not prevent it.)
  
  ## Precondition
  
  ₣BK closed: RBCC stable, moorings layout settled, RBBC aliases retired. Building the projector against an in-flight RBCC is the one thing the design explicitly forbids.
  
  ## Locked shape
  
  - **Two manifests, two projectors.** rbz (registry/colophons) → a generic BUK emitter over the zipper rolls; RBCC (flat tinder: moorings dir, `.env` filenames, roles) → an RBK projector. BUK provides mechanism and stays RBK-ignorant; RBK owns domain content. Dependency points RBK→BUK only. The proven precedent is `buz_emit_context`, which already generates the markdown command reference from the same kindled rolls — the Rust emitter is a sibling target, not new machinery.
  - **Naming: carry the enroll varname verbatim.** The rbz enroll varname is the cross-language symbol; the generated Rust const takes that exact name, so BUK names nothing. Retires the curated `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` names (a rename across their reference sites).
  - **Scope gate: single canonical bash author, or out.** Colophons and RBCC tinder qualify. Fixtures / operations / container-roles are out — no single bash author (held across `rbte_engine.sh` arrays, disk imprints, and Rust).
  - **Engine change (BUK):** `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a roll; today it is `printf -v`'d into the caller and discarded, so the registry has no stable symbol for the emitter to name consts after.
  - **Wiring:** generated file checked in; regenerated at the BUK-available layer before every Rust build; a build inside a minimal container consumes the committed artifact (never regenerates); a `git diff --exit-code` qualify gate makes a stale commit fail loud. This supersedes `rbtdrm_verify`'s drift role with exact equality.
  
  ## What done looks like
  
  - The rbtd colophon consts and the RBCC-derived consts are generated, not hand-written; `rbtdrm_verify` is retired; inline colophon literals (e.g. in the fast suite) reference the generated consts; the diff-gate is wired into qualify.
  
  ## Sources
  
  - `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_emit_context` is the bash→artifact template; engine that needs the varname roll
  - `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` — colophon registry (one source)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh` — RBCC flat tinder (the other source; already self-described as projection inventory)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — current hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_verify` (the target to replace)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/lib.rs` — `rbtd_moorings_dir!` macro (already-landed single-source precedent)

file.content:
## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it (the zipper registry, RBCC flat tinder). Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no constant is hand-maintained on both sides of a language boundary that a single canonical source could feed.

## ₢BPAAB — cross-language-constant-codegen

**Goal in one sentence:** Generate the bash↔Rust constant mirrors from canonical bash at build time, so drift becomes structurally impossible — replacing today's hand-copied Rust mirror + runtime `rbtdrm_verify` drift-*detector* with exact-equality codegen that *prevents* drift.

**Heat context:** rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup (₣BP) — "consolidate to the single rightful owner." This is pace 2 of 2; ₢BPAAA (ifrit Claude-Code retirement) already landed.

**Name assessment:** silks `cross-language-constant-codegen` fits the docket exactly — no rename needed.

**What I found in the sources (grounding the cut):**
- `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` holds three const families: ~70 hand-mirrored `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` (✅ in scope — single author = rbz), plus `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` / `RBTDRM_OPERATION_*` / `RBTDRM_CONTAINER_*` (❌ out — no single bash author, stay hand-written). `rbtdrm_verify` walks 11 Rust consumers.
- `buz_enroll` (rbz_zipper line 141) does `printf -v` the colophon into the caller and **discards the varname** — so the registry has no stable symbol to name consts after. This is the engine gap the locked shape calls out.
- `buz_emit_context` is the proven bash→artifact precedent (sibling target confirmed).
- `lib.rs` already single-sources `RBCC_moorings_dir` via the `rbtd_moorings_dir!` macro — the RBCC projector supersedes that hand-mirror. No `build.rs` exists yet.

**This pace is flagged "decompose into sub-paces at groom."** The dependency tree is clean. My proposed cut (pace order = dependency order):

| # | Sub-pace | Layer | Depends on |
|---|----------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | `buz-enroll-varname-roll` — retain enroll varname in a new roll | BUK engine | — |
| 2 | `buz-rust-emitter` — generic Rust-const emitter over zipper rolls, sibling to `buz_emit_context` | BUK mechanism | 1 |

content.text:
Here's my honest read.

**I recommend substeps — and I'd be uncomfortable doing this monolithically.** The reasoning, specifically:

**Three different risk classes in one pace.** This isn't a uniform mechanical change. It's (a) *novel cross-language mechanism* — the `buz_enroll` varname roll and the Rust emitter don't exist yet and need design judgment; (b) an *11-site consumer rename* with retirement of `rbtdrm_verify` — broad blast radius, mechanical but easy to miss a site; and (c) *qualify-infrastructure wiring* — the "regenerate before build, but never inside the container, gated by `git diff --exit-code`" distinction is exactly the kind of subtle ordering that's easy to get half-right. Bundling all three into one thread means a failure in (c) sits on top of unverified (a) and (b).

**Each layer has a natural verification gate.** BUK self-test green → Rust builds + fast suite green → moorings paths intact → qualify gate actually fails on a stale commit. Substeps put a `jjx_record` checkpoint at each of those, which means clean per-layer commits and granular rollback. Monolithic gives me one giant commit (or ad-hoc notching) and no clean recovery point if the gate wiring misbehaves.

**It survives context compaction.** This is a lot of files across BUK + RBK + Rust + qualify. A single mounted thread risks me losing the thread mid-way; sub-pace dockets re-anchor each segment.

**The coupling argument *for* monolithic is real but weak here.** Yes, the emitter's output shape determines the consumer migration — but substeps don't force premature commitment. You design step 2's output knowing step 3 will consume it; the boundary is just a checkpoint, not a wall.

**One refinement to my proposed cut:** I'd merge the two BUK steps (varname roll + emitter) into one — the roll exists *only* to feed the emitter, and the `buz_enroll` change is purely additive (we add a roll; `printf -v` stays, so existing callers are untouched). That gives a tighter **4 sub-paces**:

1. **BUK mechanism** — varname roll + generic Rust emitter (gate: emitter emits correct consts from rbz rolls; BUK self-test green)
2. **Colophon projection** — wire rbz→emitter, migrate 11 consumers, retire `rbtdrm_verify` (gate: fast suite green, Rust builds)
3. **RBCC tinder projection** — supersede `rbtd_moorings_dir!` macro (gate: builds, moorings paths intact)
4. **Wiring + diff-gate** — regen hook, container consumes committed artifact, qualify gate (gate: stale commit fails loud)

My recommendation: **groom into these 4.** Want me to enroll them?
I'll enroll the 4 sub-paces, then drop the rough ₢BPAAB. Each enroll needs its own gazette write. Starting with sub-pace 1.

content.text:
Good instinct to review before mounting. I re-read the original ₢BPAAB locked shape against the four dockets I wrote. Here's what I found, ordered by how much it could steer the work wrong.

## Substantive — could cause wrong or gapped work

**1. `rbtdrm_verify` retirement is in the wrong sub-pace (BPAAD).**
The original locked shape says the diff-gate "*supersedes `rbtdrm_verify`'s drift role with exact equality*" — i.e. the gate is what *replaces* verify. But I put "retire `rbtdrm_verify`" in BPAAD (colophon migration), while the diff-gate doesn't land until BPAAF. That opens a window across BPAAD→BPAAE where there is **no drift protection at all** — hand-edits to the generated file would go undetected.

There's also a coupling I glossed: `rbtdrm_verify` reads `rbtdrm_required_colophons()`, whose arrays reference the `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*` consts. When BPAAD renames those consts to the generated names, that map's references must update — but verify itself can stay alive (repointed) until the gate replaces it.

*Suggested fix:* BPAAD repoints `rbtdrm_required_colophons` to the generated names but keeps `rbtdrm_verify` running; BPAAF retires `rbtdrm_verify` (and likely its now-dead `rbtdrm_required_colophons` map) at the moment the diff-gate takes over. Continuous protection, and it matches the original's "supersedes" wording.

**2. BPAAE's projection scope may conflict with the heat's own theme.**
The paddock theme is "remove constants *redundantly held in more than one place*." Today the **only** RBCC constant with an actual Rust mirror is `RBCC_moorings_dir` (the `rbtd_moorings_dir!` macro). The `.env` filenames and roles live *only* in bash — they're already single-homed. Generating Rust consts for them would *create* a second home, the opposite of the heat's goal — yet the original locked shape explicitly names "moorings dir, .env filenames, roles" as the projector's input.

That's a real tension between the pace shape and the heat theme, and my docket buried it under vague "single-canonical-author source-time literals only." *Suggested fix:* make BPAAE's docket name the open question explicitly — "project the full named tinder set, or only constants that have a Rust mirror today?" — for mount-time resolution rather than silently deciding it.

**3. The "two projectors" relationship is unresolved.**
The original says rbz→"a generic BUK emitter" and RBCC→"an RBK projector," but also "BUK provides mechanism … RBK owns domain content." It's ambiguous whether BPAAE's RBK projector **reuses** BPAAC's BUK emitter (feeding RBCC values in as rolls) or is **standalone** codegen. This determines whether BPAAE actually depends on BPAAC, and whether BPAAC's emitter needs to accept non-zipper input. My dockets quietly assume standalone. *Suggested fix:* surface the choice in BPAAC/BPAAE rather than presuming.

## Discipline / precision

**4. "11-site" in BPAAD violates the stale-by-mount filter.**
JJK docket discipline forbids match counts (drift-prone). Worse, my "11" came from a *combined* grep of `RBTDRM_COLOPHON` **or** `rbtdrm_verify` across files — it conflates const-consumers with verify-callers, so it's not even an accurate rename-site count. *Suggested fix:* drop the number; the docket already carries the grep recipe — let that find the sites.

**5. BPAAC's gate ("BUK self-test green") doesn't actually verify the emitter.**
The BUK self-test exercises the test framework (kick-tires + bure-tweak), not the zipper emitter. Green there is a regression guard, not proof the emitter emits correct consts. *Suggested fix:* state the real acceptance — run the emitter and compare its output to the expected consts — with self-test as a no-regression check.

## Minor

**6.** BPAAE sits after BPAAD but (per #3, if standalone) doesn't depend on it — they're independent siblings. Harmless for sequential execution; just noting the order isn't a true dependency.

---

Points **1, 2, and 4** are the ones I'd insist on; 3 and 5 are clarifications that save the mount agent a wrong turn. Want me to redocket BPAAC, BPAAD, BPAAE, and BPAAF with these corrections folded in? I'd do it as a single mass reslate.

## Character

Novel BUK mechanism — design judgment lives in the emitter shape; the engine change is additive and low-risk. RBK-ignorant: BUK provides mechanism only, names nothing of its own.

## Goal

Give the zipper registry a stable cross-language symbol per enrolled colophon, and a generic emitter that projects those symbols to Rust consts — mirroring the existing bash-to-artifact pattern of `buz_emit_context`.

## Shape

- `buz_enroll` must retain the enroll varname in a new roll. Today it `printf -v`'s the colophon into the caller and discards the varname, so the registry has no stable symbol for an emitter to name consts after. Additive: `printf -v` stays, existing callers untouched.
- Add a generic Rust-const emitter (sibling target to `buz_emit_context`) over the kindled rolls. The generated const name is the enroll varname verbatim.
- Open design question to settle here: does this emitter stay zipper-roll-only (the RBCC projector in the next paces being separate machinery), or is it shaped to accept arbitrary name/value pairs so the RBK RBCC projector can reuse it? The original locked shape says "BUK provides mechanism, RBK owns domain content" but also frames them as two distinct projectors — resolve before building.

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — `buz_enroll` (varname discard site) and `buz_emit_context` (the bash-to-artifact template)

## What done looks like

- A roll carries enroll varnames; a BUK emitter turns the rolls into `pub const NAME: &str = "...";` lines.
- Acceptance is the emitter's own output: run it against the rbz rolls and confirm the emitted consts match the expected colophons. The BUK self-test is a no-regression check, not emitter verification.

# jjezs_reslate BPAAD

## Character

Mechanical once the emitter exists, but broad — a consumer rename across the rbtd Rust sources.

## Goal

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` — colophon registry
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_verify` + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
